title: Quiz: Quantum Feature Maps
description: Test your understanding of quantum feature maps

questions.question: What is the typical structure of a ZZ-feature map?
questions.explanation: The ZZ-feature map typically starts with Hadamard gates to create superposition, followed by single-qubit Z rotations encoding the data, and then ZZ interactions (entangling gates) that encode pairwise feature products.

questions.question: What does increasing the number of repetitions (reps) in a feature map do?

options.text: Increases expressibility but also circuit depth

questions.explanation: More repetitions increase the expressibility of the feature map, potentially enabling more complex decision boundaries. However, this also increases circuit depth, which can lead to more noise on real hardware.

questions.question: What makes IQP (Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial) circuits special?

options.text: They use diagonal gates sandwiched between Hadamards

questions.explanation: IQP circuits have the structure H⊗n → Diagonal → H⊗n, where the diagonal gates apply phases based on the computational basis states. This structure is believed to be hard to simulate classically for random instances.

questions.question: A feature map using only single-qubit rotations (no entanglement) can still be useful for quantum kernel methods.
questions.explanation: True. Product state feature maps (no entanglement) can still provide useful kernels, especially for data where features are approximately independent. However, entanglement generally enables richer feature interactions.

questions.question: Why might a kernel matrix with very small off-diagonal values be problematic?

options.text: It makes all data points appear equally dissimilar

questions.explanation: Very small off-diagonal values mean all data points appear nearly orthogonal (maximally dissimilar). This makes it hard for kernel methods to find meaningful patterns, as there's little structure in the similarity relationships.
